title: Creating Matrices
description: Learn how to create matrices using matrix(), dim(), and understand the byrow argument.

slides.title: What is a Matrix? 🧮
slides.content:
# Matrices

A **matrix** is a two-dimensional rectangular data structure. Like vectors, all elements must be of the same type (numeric, character, logical, etc.).

Think of a matrix as a table with rows and columns.

```r
# A numeric matrix
     [,1] [,2] [,3]
[1,]    1    3    5
[2,]    2    4    6
```

Matrices are fundamental in linear algebra and data analysis.

slides.title: Creating with matrix()
slides.content:
The primary function is `matrix()`. Basic syntax:

```r
matrix(data, nrow, ncol, byrow = FALSE)
```

- `data`: a vector of elements.
- `nrow`: number of rows.
- `ncol`: number of columns.
- `byrow`: if `TRUE`, fill row-wise; default `FALSE` fills column-wise.

```r
m1 <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)
# Fills column-wise:
#      [,1] [,2] [,3]
# [1,]    1    3    5
# [2,]    2    4    6

m2 <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)
# Fills row-wise:
#      [,1] [,2] [,3]
# [1,]    1    2    3
# [2,]    4    5    6
```

slides.title: Create Matrices
slides.codeSnippet:
m1 <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)
print(m1)

m2 <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)
print(m2)

quizData.question: What is the value in row 2, column 2 of matrix `matrix(1:9, nrow = 3, byrow = FALSE)`?
quizData.explanation: Column-wise fill: first column: 1,2,3; second column: 4,5,6. So row2, col2 = 5.

slides.title: Using dim() to Convert Vector to Matrix
slides.content:
You can also assign a `dim` attribute to a vector to turn it into a matrix.

```r
x <- 1:6
dim(x) <- c(2, 3)
x
```

This modifies the vector in place, giving it dimensions.

slides.title: dim() Example
slides.codeSnippet:
x <- 1:6
dim(x) <- c(2,3)
print(x)

# Check class
print(class(x))

quizData.question: What happens when you assign `dim(x) <- c(3,2)` to a vector of length 6?
quizData.explanation: As long as the product of dimensions equals the length, you can reshape any vector into a matrix.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: You can create a matrix with a single value repeated using `matrix(0, nrow=3, ncol=4)` for a zero matrix, or `matrix(NA, nrow=2, ncol=2)` for missing values.
